S02.meta: // SLIDE 02 — WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO.
blocks: · Write prompts that produce a usable data dictionary review rather than a generic description
· Ask Claude to help select an analysis approach given your specific data and question
· Prompt Claude to check statistical assumptions before you commit to a test
· Get plain-language explanations of code and output without trusting Claude's arithmetic
· Draft limitations sections that are honest about what the data cannot support
data.section: WHAT THIS CHAPTER LETS YOU DO

data.title: CORE CONCEPTS
blocks: Data dictionary.
Analysis plan.
Assumption checking.
Code explanation versus calculation verification.
Limitations paragraph.

data.title: BEFORE / AFTER PROMPT WALKTHROUGH.
blocks: Variable definitions are explicit Claude now knows what each variable is and is not.
The research question is stated no need for Claude to invent one.
The task is sequenced assumption review before test recommendation, not the reverse.
The constraint is named Claude is told what it cannot know and required to flag it.

S05.meta: // SLIDE 05 — THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT YOU MUST

data.title: THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT YOU MUST VERIFY
blocks: Verify every number independently.
Verify causal language.
Verify assumption checks against your data.
Verify what "significant" means in context.
data.section: THE HUMAN GATE: WHAT YOU MUST

data.title: COMMON MISTAKES
blocks: Asking Claude to interpret data it has not been given.
Using Claude's output as proof the analysis is correct.
Asking "what does this mean?" instead of "what did I measure?"
Treating a limitations paragraph as boilerplate.
Skipping the analysis plan.
